questions.question: What change would most effectively address this reliability issue?
questions.scenario: Production data shows that in 12% of cases, your customer support agent skips get_customer entirely and calls lookup_order using only the customer's stated name, occasionally leading to misidentified accounts and incorrect refunds. The agent's system prompt states that verification is required.
options: Implement a routing classifier that analyzes each request and enables only the subset of tools appropriate for that request type
Add a programmatic prerequisite that blocks lookup_order and process_refund calls until get_customer has returned a verified customer ID
Add few-shot examples showing the agent always calling get_customer first, even when customers volunteer order details
Enhance the system prompt to emphasize that customer verification via get_customer is mandatory before any order operations
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Programmatic enforcement provides deterministic guarantees that critical business logic is followed, regardless of prompt compliance rates.
[Why B fails]: The system prompt already states verification is required — strengthening wording doesn't address the 12% non-compliance rate for a probabilistic system.
[Why C fails]: Few-shot examples improve compliance but cannot guarantee it; financial operations require deterministic enforcement.
[Why D fails]: A routing classifier addresses tool availability, not tool ordering — the agent still has access to lookup_order, just not the prerequisite.
[Principle]: Programmatic enforcement over prompt-based for deterministic requirements.
[Doc]: Hooks Guide – Enforcement → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide
[Doc]: PreToolUse Decision Control → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#pretooluse-2
[Doc]: Hooks Reference → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks
[Doc]: SDK Hooks → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/hooks

questions.question: What is the correct loop termination behavior?
questions.scenario: You are implementing the core agentic loop for your customer support agent. After sending a request to Claude, the response contains a stop_reason field. You need to determine when to continue executing tools versus when to present the final response to the customer.
options: Continue the loop when stop_reason is 'tool_use' and terminate when stop_reason is 'end_turn'
Continue the loop when the response contains tool calls in the text, and terminate when the model says 'I'm done'
Check if the assistant's response contains a final answer pattern and terminate if found
Set a maximum iteration count of 10 and terminate when reached, regardless of stop_reason
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: The agentic loop lifecycle is defined by stop_reason — 'tool_use' means the model wants to execute tools, 'end_turn' means it's finished.
[Why B fails]: Parsing natural language signals to determine loop termination is an anti-pattern; stop_reason is the definitive signal.
[Why C fails]: Arbitrary iteration caps as the primary stopping mechanism is an anti-pattern — it may terminate too early or too late.
[Why D fails]: Checking for text content patterns is unreliable and undocumented; stop_reason is the correct mechanism.
[Principle]: Use stop_reason as the definitive loop control signal — not text parsing or arbitrary limits.
[Doc]: Agentic Systems → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works
[Doc]: Agent Loop (SDK) → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/agent-loop

questions.question: What is the most effective approach to improve escalation calibration?
questions.scenario: Your customer support agent achieves 52% first-contact resolution, well below the 80% target. Production logs reveal two patterns: (1) the agent escalates straightforward cases like standard damage replacements where photo evidence is provided and policy clearly applies, while (2) autonomously attempting to resolve complex situations requiring policy exceptions such as competitor price matching that has no documented procedure.
options: Add explicit escalation criteria to the system prompt with few-shot examples demonstrating when to escalate versus resolve autonomously
Implement sentiment analysis to detect customer frustration and automatically escalate when negative sentiment exceeds a threshold
Deploy a separate classifier model trained on historical tickets to predict which requests need escalation before the agent begins processing
Have the agent self-report a confidence score (1-10) before each action and automatically escalate when confidence falls below 5
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Explicit criteria with few-shot examples directly address unclear decision boundaries — the proportionate first response before adding infrastructure.
[Why B fails]: LLM self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated; the agent is already incorrectly confident on hard cases and incorrectly uncertain on easy ones.
[Why C fails]: Over-engineered — requires labeled data and ML infrastructure when prompt optimization hasn't been tried yet.
[Why D fails]: Sentiment doesn't correlate with case complexity; frustrated customers may have simple issues, calm customers may have complex ones.
[Principle]: Address unclear decision boundaries with explicit criteria before adding infrastructure.
[Doc]: Agentic Systems – Escalation → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works
[Doc]: Prompt Engineering → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview

questions.question: What is the most appropriate action?
questions.scenario: A customer contacts your support agent saying: 'I want to speak to a real person. I've been dealing with this billing issue for a week and I'm done with bots.' Your agent has access to the customer's account and can see the billing discrepancy is a straightforward duplicate charge that it could resolve in one tool call.
options: Acknowledge the frustration but explain you can resolve the duplicate charge right now, then attempt resolution before offering escalation
Immediately escalate to a human agent with a structured handoff summary including customer ID, the duplicate charge details, and recommended resolution
Escalate to a human but first investigate the billing issue to provide the human agent with a complete analysis
Ask the customer clarifying questions about the billing issue to determine if escalation is truly necessary
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: When a customer explicitly requests a human agent, honor that request immediately without attempting further investigation — include structured context for the human.
[Why B fails]: The customer explicitly said 'I want to speak to a real person' and 'I'm done with bots' — attempting resolution ignores their stated preference.
[Why C fails]: Investigating first delays the escalation the customer explicitly requested; the human agent can investigate themselves.
[Why D fails]: Asking clarifying questions directly contradicts the customer's explicit request to stop interacting with the bot.
[Principle]: Honor explicit customer requests for human agents immediately without first attempting investigation.
[Doc]: Agentic Systems – Escalation → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works

questions.question: What is the most effective implementation?
questions.scenario: Your customer support agent processes refunds up to $500 per company policy. However, production logs show that in 3% of cases, the agent processes refunds between $500 and $800 when customers provide compelling emotional narratives. The system prompt states the $500 limit clearly, but prompt compliance is not 100%.
options: Implement a tool call interception hook that blocks process_refund when the amount exceeds $500 and redirects to human escalation with the refund details
Add few-shot examples showing the agent correctly declining refunds above $500 and escalating instead
Reduce the agent's autonomy by requiring human approval for all refunds regardless of amount
Add stronger wording to the system prompt: 'UNDER NO CIRCUMSTANCES process refunds exceeding $500 — this is a fireable policy violation'
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: A hook that intercepts the tool call provides deterministic enforcement of business rules — the refund physically cannot be processed above the threshold.
[Why B fails]: Stronger prompt wording still relies on probabilistic LLM compliance; 3% failure rate on a financial rule is unacceptable regardless of emphasis.
[Why C fails]: Few-shot examples improve but don't guarantee compliance — still probabilistic for a deterministic requirement.
[Why D fails]: Requiring human approval for all refunds eliminates the 80%+ resolution benefit of automation; it's disproportionate to a 3% edge case.
[Principle]: Use hooks over prompt-based enforcement when business rules require guaranteed compliance.
[Doc]: Hooks Reference → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks
[Doc]: SDK Hooks → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/hooks
[Doc]: Hooks Guide – Interception → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide
[Doc]: PreToolUse Hook → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#pretooluse-2

questions.question: How should the agent handle this situation?
questions.scenario: A customer asks your support agent to match a competitor's price that is 30% lower than your listed price. Your company's return/refund policy is documented and covers standard adjustments, but says nothing about competitor price matching. The agent has tools for standard price adjustments but no explicit 'match competitor price' capability.
options: Decline the request and inform the customer that competitor price matching is not available, citing the existing policy
Escalate to a human agent because the policy is silent on competitor price matching — this represents a policy gap that requires human judgment
Apply the standard price adjustment tool to reduce the price by 30%, since the customer has a valid request and the tool technically supports it
Ask the customer for proof of the competitor's price and then attempt to apply the adjustment using available tools
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: When policy is ambiguous or silent on a specific request, the agent should escalate rather than making autonomous decisions that may violate unstated company intent.
[Why B fails]: Using available tools for an undocumented scenario risks unauthorized discounts; tool capability doesn't imply policy authorization.
[Why C fails]: The agent doesn't know that price matching isn't available — it only knows the policy doesn't address it. Declining definitively oversteps.
[Why D fails]: Gathering evidence implies intent to process the match, which isn't authorized by existing policy.
[Principle]: Escalate when policy is ambiguous or silent on the customer's specific request.
[Doc]: Agentic Systems – Escalation → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works

questions.question: What's the most effective first step to improve tool selection reliability?
questions.scenario: Production logs show your customer support agent frequently calls get_customer when users ask about orders (e.g., 'check my order #12345'), instead of calling lookup_order. Both tools have minimal descriptions — 'Retrieves customer information' and 'Retrieves order details' respectively — and both accept similar identifier formats.
options: Consolidate both tools into a single lookup_entity tool that accepts any identifier and internally determines which backend to query
Expand each tool's description to include input formats, example queries, edge cases, and boundaries explaining when to use it versus similar tools
Add 5-8 few-shot examples to the system prompt demonstrating correct tool selection for order-related queries
Implement a routing layer that parses user input before each turn and pre-selects the appropriate tool based on detected keywords
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Tool descriptions are the primary mechanism LLMs use for tool selection. Minimal descriptions are the root cause — expanding them is a low-effort, high-leverage fix.
[Why B fails]: Few-shot examples add token overhead without fixing the underlying issue; they treat the symptom (wrong choices) not the cause (inadequate descriptions).
[Why C fails]: A routing layer is over-engineered and bypasses the LLM's natural language understanding, adding infrastructure before trying the simpler fix.
[Why D fails]: Consolidating tools is a valid architectural choice but requires more effort than warranted as a 'first step' when the immediate problem is inadequate descriptions.
[Principle]: Tool descriptions are the primary mechanism for tool selection — fix descriptions before adding infrastructure.
[Doc]: Tool Definitions Best Practices → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/define-tools#best-practices-for-tool-definitions
[Doc]: Custom Tools (SDK) → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/custom-tools

questions.question: How should the error response be structured?
questions.scenario: Your get_customer MCP tool returns a generic 'Operation failed' message when it cannot find a customer. However, there are three distinct failure modes: (1) the customer email has a typo, (2) the customer account was merged into another, and (3) the customer service is temporarily unavailable. Your agent currently retries all failures identically, wasting 3 attempts on non-retryable errors.
options: Add retry logic with exponential backoff inside the tool itself, only surfacing errors after all retries are exhausted
Return a standardized HTTP status code (404, 503, etc.) that the agent can use to determine the appropriate response
Return the raw error message from the backend system so the agent can parse it and make decisions
Return structured error metadata including errorCategory (transient/validation/business), isRetryable boolean, and a human-readable description for each failure mode
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Structured error metadata with explicit categories and retryable flags gives the agent the information needed for appropriate recovery decisions.
[Why B fails]: Raw backend messages are inconsistent and require fragile parsing; structured metadata is more reliable for agent decision-making.
[Why C fails]: Internal retry with generic surfacing hides valuable context from the agent — it can't explain to the customer what happened or make recovery decisions.
[Why D fails]: HTTP status codes are designed for web APIs, not LLM tool interactions; they lack the semantic richness needed (e.g., 404 doesn't distinguish typo from merged account).
[Principle]: Structured error context enables intelligent agent recovery decisions.
[Doc]: Custom Tool Error Handling → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/custom-tools#handle-errors
[Doc]: Tool Use Error Handling → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/handle-tool-calls#handling-errors-with-is_error

questions.question: What data should the hook normalize before the agent processes it?
questions.scenario: Your customer support agent uses three MCP tools that return order data in different formats: get_order returns dates as Unix timestamps (1716393600), lookup_history returns dates as ISO 8601 strings ('2025-05-22T12:00:00Z'), and check_status returns dates as 'May 22, 2025'. The agent occasionally confuses timestamps with order IDs and gives customers incorrect date information.
options: Create a wrapper tool that calls each underlying tool and normalizes the output before returning it to the agent
Add instructions to the system prompt explaining the three date formats and how to interpret each one
Update all three tools to return a consistent format, requiring backend changes across three different services
Implement a PostToolUse hook that normalizes all date formats to ISO 8601 and all status codes to human-readable strings before the agent processes the results
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: PostToolUse hooks intercept tool results for transformation before the model processes them — ideal for normalizing heterogeneous formats without changing tools.
[Why B fails]: Modifying three backend services for agent format consistency is disproportionate effort and may break other consumers of those APIs.
[Why C fails]: Relying on prompt instructions for format interpretation is error-prone; the agent already confuses timestamps with IDs, showing instructions aren't sufficient.
[Why D fails]: A wrapper tool adds an unnecessary layer when hooks already provide the interception point; it also requires maintaining wrapper logic per tool.
[Principle]: Use PostToolUse hooks to normalize heterogeneous data formats from different tools before agent processing.
[Doc]: Hooks Reference → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks
[Doc]: SDK Hooks → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/hooks
[Doc]: PostToolUse Hook → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks#posttooluse

questions.question: What context management strategy would most effectively address this issue?
questions.scenario: Your customer support agent handles multi-issue sessions where customers raise 3-4 concerns. After resolving the first two issues across 8 tool calls, the agent starts referencing 'your previous order' vaguely instead of specific order IDs and amounts. The tool results from early calls (containing 40+ fields each) have accumulated in context, and key transactional facts are being lost in progressive summarization.
options: Extract transactional facts (order IDs, amounts, dates, statuses) into a persistent 'case facts' block included in each prompt, outside the summarized conversation history
Trim all tool results to only 5 fields before they enter the conversation history
Increase the model's max_tokens to accommodate the full conversation without summarization
Start a fresh session for each new issue the customer raises, passing only the customer ID
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: A persistent 'case facts' block preserves critical transactional data outside the summarization pipeline, ensuring specifics aren't lost as conversation grows.
[Why B fails]: max_tokens controls output length, not context window; even with full context, the 'lost in the middle' effect means specifics from early interactions may be missed.
[Why C fails]: Aggressive trimming to 5 fields may discard information needed for later issues; the problem isn't field count but fact persistence across summarization boundaries.
[Why D fails]: Fresh sessions lose cross-issue context (e.g., 'while you're fixing my order, can you also check my subscription?') and force re-verification.
[Principle]: Extract and persist structured transactional facts outside summarized history to prevent progressive information loss.
[Doc]: Context Window Management → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/context-window
[Doc]: Context Window (SDK) → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/agent-loop#the-context-window
[Doc]: Compaction & Fact Preservation → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/agent-loop#automatic-compaction
[Doc]: Memory & CLAUDE.md → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory

questions.question: What should the agent do?
questions.scenario: Your customer support agent calls get_customer with an email address and receives two matching results: 'Sarah Chen (ID: 4521, joined 2019)' and 'Sarah Chen (ID: 8903, joined 2023)'. The customer said 'Hi, I'm Sarah Chen and I need help with my recent order.'
options: Select the account with the more recent join date (ID: 8903) since the customer mentioned a 'recent' order
Proceed with both accounts and present order information from each, letting the customer identify which is theirs
Select the account with the earlier join date (ID: 4521) since it's likely the primary account
Ask the customer for additional identifying information (e.g., email address, last 4 digits of phone number) to disambiguate between the two accounts
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: When tool results return multiple matches, request additional identifiers for disambiguation rather than selecting based on heuristics.
[Why B fails]: 'Recent order' refers to the order timing, not account age; heuristic selection risks acting on the wrong account.
[Why C fails]: Assuming the older account is primary is an unjustified heuristic that could lead to unauthorized access.
[Why D fails]: Presenting information from both accounts may expose one customer's data to another person, creating a privacy/security risk.
[Principle]: Request additional identifiers when multiple matches exist rather than selecting based on heuristics.
[Doc]: Ambiguity Resolution Patterns → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works
[Doc]: Tool Definitions – Disambiguation → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/define-tools#best-practices-for-tool-definitions

questions.question: What information should the handoff summary contain?
questions.scenario: Your agent determines it cannot resolve a customer's warranty extension request because the situation falls outside documented policy. It needs to escalate to a human agent. The human agent will not have access to the conversation transcript and must pick up the case cold.
options: The customer's emotional state assessment, satisfaction score, and a priority level rating to help the human agent prioritize

A structured summary with: customer ID, verified account details, root cause analysis (warranty expired 3 days ago, customer claims item was defective from purchase), policy gap identified, recommended action, and all relevant order/product details
A brief message: 'Customer needs warranty extension — please assist' with the customer ID
The full conversation transcript between the customer and the agent, allowing the human to read through the entire interaction
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Structured handoff summaries must include customer details, root cause, recommended action, and all context the human needs to act without the transcript.
[Why B fails]: The transcript isn't available to the human agent (as stated in the scenario), and even if it were, parsing a long conversation is inefficient.
[Why C fails]: Too brief — the human agent would need to re-investigate everything from scratch, increasing resolution time.
[Why D fails]: Emotional assessments and priority scores are subjective and less actionable than factual case details; they don't help the human resolve the issue.
[Principle]: Compile structured handoff summaries with factual context when escalating to agents who lack conversation access.
[Doc]: Agent Handoff Patterns → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works

questions.question: What is the most likely root cause?
questions.scenario: Your multi-agent research system produces a report on 'impact of AI on creative industries.' Each subagent completes successfully — the web search agent finds articles, the document analysis agent summarizes papers correctly, and the synthesis agent produces coherent output. However, the final report covers only visual arts (digital art, graphic design, photography), completely missing music, writing, and film. The coordinator's logs show it decomposed the topic into: 'AI in digital art creation,' 'AI in graphic design,' and 'AI in photography.'
options: The document analysis agent is filtering out sources related to non-visual creative industries due to restrictive relevance criteria
The coordinator agent's task decomposition is too narrow, resulting in subagent assignments that don't cover all relevant domains of the topic
The synthesis agent lacks instructions for identifying coverage gaps in the findings it receives from other agents
The web search agent's queries are not comprehensive enough and need to be expanded to cover more sectors
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: The coordinator's logs reveal the root cause directly — it decomposed 'creative industries' into only visual arts subtasks, missing music, writing, and film entirely.
[Why B fails]: The synthesis agent correctly synthesized what it received; the problem is upstream at task decomposition, not downstream at synthesis.
[Why C fails]: The web search agent executed its assigned tasks correctly — it searched for what it was told to search for.
[Why D fails]: The document analysis agent analyzed what it received; there's no evidence of filtering since it never received non-visual content.
[Principle]: When all subagents succeed but output is incomplete, trace the problem to task decomposition at the coordinator level.
[Doc]: Subagent Task Delegation → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/subagents
[Doc]: Agentic Systems – Orchestration → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works
[Doc]: Agents Overview → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agents

questions.question: What is the most effective approach to reduce overhead while maintaining reliability?
questions.scenario: During testing, you observe that the synthesis agent frequently needs to verify specific claims while combining findings. Currently, each verification requires a round trip: synthesis → coordinator → web search → coordinator → synthesis. This adds 2-3 round trips per task, increasing latency by 40%. Your evaluation shows 85% of verifications are simple fact-checks (dates, names, statistics) while 15% require deeper investigation.
options: Have the synthesis agent accumulate all verification needs and return them as a batch to the coordinator at the end of its pass
Give the synthesis agent a scoped verify_fact tool for simple lookups, while complex verifications continue through the coordinator to the web search agent
Have the web search agent proactively include extra context around each source during initial research, anticipating verification needs
Give the synthesis agent full access to all web search tools so it can handle any verification directly without coordinator involvement
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: A scoped tool for the common case (85% simple lookups) reduces latency while preserving coordinator oversight for complex cases — least privilege with practical optimization.
[Why B fails]: Batching creates blocking dependencies since synthesis steps may depend on earlier verified facts; you can't finish synthesis without intermediate verifications.
[Why C fails]: Over-provisions the synthesis agent with tools outside its specialization, violating separation of concerns and degrading tool selection reliability.
[Why D fails]: Speculative caching cannot reliably predict what the synthesis agent will need to verify; it wastes tokens on unused context.
[Principle]: Provide scoped cross-role tools for high-frequency simple needs while routing complex cases through the coordinator.
[Doc]: Agent Tools Configuration → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/subagents#agentdefinition-configuration
[Doc]: Tools Reference → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/tools-reference
[Doc]: Permissions (SDK) → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/permissions
[Doc]: Subagent Least Privilege → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#control-subagent-capabilities

questions.question: How should you improve the system?
questions.scenario: Your multi-agent research system's synthesis output frequently lacks source citations. Investigation reveals: (1) the web search agent returns findings as prose paragraphs summarizing multiple sources together, (2) the document analysis agent returns bullet points without source attribution, and (3) the synthesis agent combines these into a report but cannot trace which claims came from which sources.
options: Have the coordinator inject all raw source URLs into the synthesis agent's prompt so it can reference them
Require subagents to output structured claim-source mappings that separate content from metadata (source URL, document name, page number, publication date) for each finding
Add a post-processing step that uses a separate Claude instance to search the internet and add citations to the finished report
Instruct the synthesis agent to include 'Source: [search results]' after each paragraph to indicate provenance
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Structured claim-source mappings preserve attribution through the pipeline, enabling the synthesis agent to maintain provenance when combining findings.
[Why B fails]: Retroactive citation search may find different sources than those actually used, introducing inaccurate attribution.
[Why C fails]: Generic 'Source: [search results]' provides no specific attribution; the synthesis agent doesn't have the source details to cite specifically.
[Why D fails]: Raw URLs without claim-level mapping don't help the synthesis agent know which URL supports which specific claim.
[Principle]: Preserve information provenance through structured claim-source mappings at every pipeline stage.
[Doc]: Source Attribution & Provenance → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/retrieval-augmented-generation

questions.question: What is the correct approach for passing context to the synthesis subagent?
questions.scenario: Your coordinator has completed the research phase: the web search agent found 12 relevant articles and the document analysis agent produced summaries of 5 academic papers. Now the coordinator needs to invoke the synthesis subagent to combine these findings into a coherent report. The synthesis agent runs with isolated context.
options: Include the complete findings from both prior agents directly in the synthesis subagent's prompt, with structured formatting separating content from metadata
Pass a reference ID that the synthesis agent can use to look up the findings from a shared memory store
Let the synthesis agent inherit the coordinator's full conversation history so it has access to all prior tool results
Include only a brief summary of findings in the prompt, trusting the synthesis agent to fill in details from its training knowledge
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Subagent context must be explicitly provided in the prompt — subagents do not automatically inherit parent context or share memory between invocations.
[Why B fails]: There is no shared memory store between agents in the Claude Agent SDK; subagents operate with isolated context.
[Why C fails]: Brief summaries lose detail and the synthesis agent would hallucinate specifics from training data rather than citing actual research findings.
[Why D fails]: Subagents do not automatically inherit the coordinator's conversation history — context isolation is a fundamental property of the architecture.
[Principle]: Subagent context must be explicitly provided — they do not inherit parent context automatically.
[Doc]: Context Passing to Subagents → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/subagents#what-subagents-inherit
[Doc]: Efficient Context → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/agent-loop#keep-context-efficient
[Doc]: Subagents Reference → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents

questions.question: What configuration is required for the coordinator to spawn subagents?
questions.scenario: You are configuring a coordinator agent that needs to delegate research tasks to specialized subagents (web search, document analysis, synthesis). The coordinator's current configuration allows it to call MCP tools directly but it cannot spawn subagents.
options: Add 'Task' to the coordinator's allowedTools configuration so it can invoke the Task tool to spawn subagents
Configure a message bus that connects the coordinator to subagent endpoints via webhooks
Import the subagent modules into the coordinator's runtime environment so they share the same process
Set the coordinator's mode to 'orchestrator' in the AgentDefinition to enable multi-agent capabilities
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: The Task tool is the mechanism for spawning subagents, and allowedTools must include 'Task' for a coordinator to invoke them.
[Why B fails]: There is no 'orchestrator' mode in AgentDefinition — subagent spawning is controlled by tool access, not mode settings.
[Why C fails]: Subagents run in isolated contexts, not shared processes; importing modules doesn't enable the spawning mechanism.
[Why D fails]: The Agent SDK uses the Task tool pattern, not webhooks or message buses — this describes a different distributed architecture.
[Principle]: The Task tool is the spawning mechanism — allowedTools must include 'Task' for coordinator agents.
[Doc]: Agent Tool Behavior → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/tools-reference#agent-tool-behavior
[Doc]: Subagents Reference → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents
[Doc]: AgentDefinition Config → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/subagents#agentdefinition-configuration
[Doc]: Tool Permissions → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/tools-reference#configure-tools-with-permission-rules-and-hooks

questions.question: How should you implement iteration to improve coverage?
questions.scenario: Your coordinator evaluates the synthesis agent's output and identifies that while the report covers market size and key players well, it has significant gaps in regulatory landscape and emerging technology trends — two of the five research dimensions specified in the original query. The web search and analysis subagents found some relevant content but it wasn't sufficient.
options: Ask the synthesis agent to expand the weak sections using its general knowledge, since it has broad training data on most topics
Spawn additional subagents focused on regulatory databases and patent filings to gather specialized source material
Return the report with annotations indicating which sections have limited coverage, letting the end user decide if they want more research

Re-delegate to the search and analysis subagents with targeted queries specifically about regulatory landscape and emerging trends, then re-invoke synthesis with the combined original and new findings
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Iterative refinement loops where the coordinator identifies gaps, re-delegates with targeted queries, and re-invokes synthesis until coverage is sufficient is the canonical pattern.
[Why B fails]: Using training knowledge for specific claims defeats the purpose of a research system — findings must be grounded in actual sources, not hallucinated.
[Why C fails]: Returning incomplete work when the system can self-improve through re-delegation is premature; the coordinator should iterate before surfacing gaps.
[Why D fails]: Spawning new specialized agents adds complexity when existing search/analysis agents can handle targeted queries about regulation and trends.
[Principle]: Implement iterative refinement — coordinator evaluates output for gaps, re-delegates with targeted queries, and re-invokes synthesis.
[Doc]: Subagent Delegation & Iteration → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/subagents
[Doc]: Agentic Systems – Orchestration Loops → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works

questions.question: What change would most improve system reliability?
questions.scenario: Your multi-agent research system gives the synthesis agent access to 18 tools: 5 web search variants, 3 document analysis tools, 4 formatting tools, 3 citation management tools, and 3 database query tools. Logs show the synthesis agent frequently misuses search tools (performing redundant searches instead of synthesizing existing findings) and occasionally calls database tools with malformed queries.
options: Add more detailed descriptions to all 18 tools explaining when the synthesis agent should and shouldn't use each one
Implement a confirmation step where the coordinator reviews each tool call from the synthesis agent before execution
Reduce the synthesis agent's tool count to 12 by consolidating the 5 search tools into 2 broader search tools
Restrict the synthesis agent's tools to only those relevant to its role — formatting, citation management, and a scoped verify_fact tool — removing search and database access
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Giving an agent access to too many tools (18 instead of 4-5) degrades selection reliability. Restricting to role-relevant tools prevents cross-specialization misuse.
[Why B fails]: With 18 tools, even perfect descriptions don't prevent selection confusion — the decision complexity itself is the problem, not just description quality.
[Why C fails]: A confirmation step adds latency to every tool call and doesn't teach the agent to stop misusing tools; it's a band-aid on a design problem.
[Why D fails]: Reducing from 18 to 12 tools doesn't address the fundamental issue — the synthesis agent shouldn't have search tools at all.
[Principle]: Restrict each agent's tool set to those relevant to its role — too many tools degrades selection reliability.
[Doc]: Agent Tools Configuration → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/subagents#agentdefinition-configuration
[Doc]: Tools Reference → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/tools-reference
[Doc]: Permissions & Scoped Access → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/permissions
[Doc]: Controlling Subagent Capabilities → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents#control-subagent-capabilities

questions.question: Which error propagation approach best enables intelligent recovery?
questions.scenario: The web search subagent times out while researching regulatory frameworks for AI in healthcare. It managed to find 3 relevant sources before the timeout occurred, but could not complete its full search scope of 8+ sources.
options: Catch the timeout and return the 3 partial results marked as a successful completion to avoid disrupting the pipeline
Return structured error context to the coordinator including the failure type (timeout), partial results (3 sources found), attempted query, and potential alternative approaches
Propagate the timeout exception directly to a top-level error handler that terminates the entire research workflow
Implement automatic retry with exponential backoff within the subagent, returning a generic 'search unavailable' status only after all retries fail
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Structured error context gives the coordinator information to make intelligent recovery decisions — retry, try alternative queries, or proceed with partial results.
[Why B fails]: Generic status hides valuable context (partial results, what was attempted) from the coordinator, preventing informed decisions.
[Why C fails]: Marking failure as success suppresses the error and prevents the coordinator from knowing coverage is incomplete — a known anti-pattern.
[Why D fails]: Terminating the entire workflow for a single subagent timeout is disproportionate when recovery strategies exist.
[Principle]: Return structured error context (type, partial results, alternatives) to enable intelligent coordinator recovery.
[Doc]: Error Propagation & Handling → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/custom-tools#handle-errors
[Doc]: Agentic Systems – Error Recovery → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works

questions.question: How should the synthesis agent handle this situation?
questions.scenario: While synthesizing findings about global AI spending, the synthesis agent encounters conflicting statistics: Source A (McKinsey, 2024) reports '$150B global AI spending' while Source B (Gartner, 2025) reports '$200B global AI spending.' Both are credible research firms. The difference could be due to different measurement years, different scope definitions, or different methodologies.
options: Average the two values ($175B) and cite both sources as supporting the figure
Flag the discrepancy as an error and exclude both statistics from the report to avoid presenting incorrect information
Include both values in the report with explicit source attribution and note the discrepancy, preserving the original characterizations and dates so readers can interpret the difference
Use the more recent source (Gartner, 2025) since it likely reflects updated data
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Conflicting statistics from credible sources should be preserved with attribution rather than arbitrarily reconciled — temporal and methodological differences explain many apparent contradictions.
[Why B fails]: Averaging conflicting statistics creates a fabricated number that no source actually reported, misrepresenting both.
[Why C fails]: Recency doesn't resolve methodology differences; the 2024 and 2025 figures may measure different things (e.g., enterprise vs total, capex vs opex).
[Why D fails]: Excluding relevant data because sources disagree impoverishes the report; real research acknowledges uncertainty.
[Principle]: Annotate conflicts with source attribution rather than arbitrarily selecting one value — preserve temporal and methodological context.
[Doc]: Source Attribution & Provenance → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/retrieval-augmented-generation

questions.question: How should you optimize the coordinator's execution?
questions.scenario: Your coordinator needs to research three independent subtopics: market size, competitive landscape, and regulatory environment. Currently, it invokes the web search subagent for market size, waits for results, then invokes it for competitive landscape, waits again, then invokes it for regulatory environment. Total latency is 45 seconds (3 × 15 seconds each).
options: Increase the timeout for each subagent to allow faster execution by giving them more processing time
Have the coordinator emit multiple Task tool calls in a single response to spawn parallel subagents for all three subtopics simultaneously
Cache previous research results so the coordinator can skip subtopics that have been researched before
Create a single 'research_all' subagent that handles all three topics sequentially in one invocation to reduce coordinator overhead
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Spawning parallel subagents by emitting multiple Task tool calls in a single coordinator response reduces latency from sequential (45s) to parallel (~15s).
[Why B fails]: A single agent handling all topics sequentially doesn't reduce latency — it still takes 45s, just hidden inside one invocation.
[Why C fails]: Increasing timeout doesn't speed up execution; each search still takes ~15 seconds regardless of timeout setting.
[Why D fails]: Caching is irrelevant for novel research queries; this isn't about repeated requests but about parallelizing independent work.
[Principle]: Spawn parallel subagents for independent tasks by emitting multiple Task tool calls in a single coordinator response.
[Doc]: Parallel Agent Execution → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agents#run-agents-in-parallel
[Doc]: Parallel Tool Execution → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/agent-loop#parallel-tool-execution

questions.question: What routing approach should the coordinator use?
questions.scenario: A user asks your multi-agent research system: 'What is the capital of France?' This is a simple factual question that doesn't require web search, document analysis, or synthesis — the coordinator can answer it directly. However, the coordinator is configured to always delegate to the full subagent pipeline.
options: Design the coordinator to analyze query requirements and dynamically decide whether to invoke subagents or respond directly based on query complexity
Always route through the full pipeline regardless of complexity to maintain consistent behavior and auditability
Configure the coordinator to ask the user whether they want a quick answer or comprehensive research before proceeding
Add a fast-path classifier that detects simple questions and bypasses the coordinator entirely
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Coordinators should analyze query requirements and dynamically select which subagents to invoke rather than always routing through the full pipeline.
[Why B fails]: Full pipeline for trivial questions wastes time and tokens; the coordinator should use judgment about when subagents add value.
[Why C fails]: Bypassing the coordinator breaks the orchestration pattern and loses observability; the coordinator should make the routing decision itself.
[Why D fails]: Asking users to classify their own queries adds friction and assumes users know what kind of research is needed.
[Principle]: Coordinators should dynamically select subagents based on query complexity rather than always routing through the full pipeline.
[Doc]: Agents Overview → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agents
[Doc]: Subagents (SDK) → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/subagents
[Doc]: Agentic Systems – Routing → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works

questions.question: How should the synthesis output handle this coverage gap?
questions.scenario: Your synthesis agent is combining findings from three subagents. The web search agent returned structured findings for 4 of 5 topic areas but the 5th area (regulatory landscape) returned an error with partial results — only 1 of 4 intended source categories was accessible. The coordinator decided to proceed with available data rather than delay the report.
options: Present all sections equally without annotation, since partial data is still better than no data
Omit the regulatory section entirely since the data is incomplete, and note at the end that regulatory analysis was unavailable
Fill in the regulatory section using the synthesis agent's training knowledge to provide comprehensive coverage
Structure the synthesis output with coverage annotations indicating which sections are well-supported versus which have gaps due to limited source availability
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Coverage annotations let consumers know which findings are well-supported and which areas have limitations, enabling informed decisions about the report's reliability.
[Why B fails]: Omitting partial findings entirely wastes the 1 source that was found; partial coverage with annotations is more useful than no coverage.
[Why C fails]: Using training knowledge for specific claims in a research system defeats the evidence-based purpose; ungrounded claims undermine the report.
[Why D fails]: Presenting incomplete sections as equal to well-supported ones is misleading; readers need to know about confidence levels.
[Principle]: Structure synthesis output with explicit coverage annotations distinguishing well-supported findings from areas with gaps.
[Doc]: Coverage Gap Handling → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works

questions.question: What's the correct approach to run Claude Code in an automated pipeline?
questions.scenario: Your CI pipeline script runs `claude "Analyze this pull request for security issues"` but the job hangs indefinitely. Build logs indicate Claude Code is waiting for interactive input that will never arrive in the headless CI environment.
options: Set the environment variable CLAUDE_HEADLESS=true before running the command
Add the -p flag: `claude -p "Analyze this pull request for security issues"` to run in non-interactive mode
Add the --batch flag: `claude --batch "Analyze this pull request for security issues"`
Redirect stdin from /dev/null: `claude "Analyze..." < /dev/null`
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: The -p (or --print) flag is the documented way to run Claude Code in non-interactive mode — it processes the prompt, outputs the result, and exits.
[Why B fails]: CLAUDE_HEADLESS is not a real environment variable in Claude Code.
[Why C fails]: Redirecting stdin is a Unix workaround that doesn't properly address Claude Code's command interface.
[Why D fails]: --batch is not a valid Claude Code flag.
[Principle]: Use -p (--print) for non-interactive Claude Code execution in CI/CD pipelines.
[Doc]: Non-Interactive / Headless Mode → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/headless
[Doc]: GitHub Actions Integration → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/github-actions
[Doc]: CLI Reference → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/cli-reference

questions.question: How should you improve the review system's precision?
questions.scenario: Your CI code review pipeline flags 52 issues per PR on average, but developers dismiss 65% without reading them. After surveying the team, you learn that 'comment accuracy' findings (where the review flags comments that don't match code behavior) have a 70% false positive rate, while 'security vulnerability' findings have only 8% false positives. Developer trust in the entire system has eroded.
options: Add a confidence threshold — only surface findings where the model reports >90% confidence
Add a 'Was this helpful?' button on each finding to gather training data for a feedback model
Temporarily disable the high false-positive 'comment accuracy' category to restore developer trust, while writing more explicit criteria for that category before re-enabling
Reduce the total number of findings shown by randomly sampling 15 per PR
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: High false-positive categories undermine trust in accurate categories. Disabling and improving them restores overall system credibility.
[Why B fails]: LLM self-reported confidence is poorly calibrated — 'be conservative' or confidence thresholds don't reliably separate true from false positives.
[Why C fails]: Random sampling doesn't address the quality problem; it just randomly hides some good findings alongside bad ones.
[Why D fails]: Feedback buttons are useful long-term but don't restore trust immediately — developers who stopped reading won't click feedback buttons.
[Principle]: Temporarily disable high false-positive categories to restore trust, then improve with explicit criteria before re-enabling.
[Doc]: Prompt Engineering – Precision → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview

questions.question: What review criteria design would most effectively reduce false positives?
questions.scenario: Your code review system currently uses the instruction: 'Flag any code that might have issues. Be thorough and report anything suspicious.' This produces many false positives on intentional patterns like local error-handling conventions and project-specific idioms that look unusual but are correct for this codebase.
options: Reduce the model temperature to 0 to make the review deterministic and less likely to hallucinate issues
Add the instruction: 'Only report high-confidence findings. Be conservative and skip anything you're not sure about.'
Add the instruction: 'You are a senior engineer. Only flag issues that a 10x developer would consider critical.'

Replace with specific categorical criteria: report bugs, security vulnerabilities, and data race conditions; skip minor style issues, local conventions, and patterns documented in the project's CLAUDE.md
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Explicit categorical criteria defining what TO report and what to SKIP gives the model clear decision boundaries rather than vague quality thresholds.
[Why B fails]: 'Be conservative' and 'high-confidence' are too vague to improve precision — the model interprets these inconsistently.
[Why C fails]: Temperature affects randomness, not accuracy; false positives come from unclear criteria, not sampling variance.
[Why D fails]: Persona-based instructions ('senior engineer', '10x developer') don't provide actionable criteria for what constitutes a reportable issue.
[Principle]: Use explicit categorical criteria (what to report vs skip) rather than vague confidence-based filtering.
[Doc]: Prompt Engineering – Explicit Criteria → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview

questions.question: What's the most effective approach to achieve consistent output?
questions.scenario: Your CI pipeline generates structured review findings in JSON format for automated posting as inline PR comments. However, the output format is inconsistent: sometimes findings include file paths, sometimes they don't; severity varies between 'high/medium/low' and 'critical/major/minor'; and suggested fixes are sometimes present, sometimes absent. Detailed instructions describing the format have not resolved the inconsistency.
options: Increase the system prompt length with more detailed format specifications and field-by-field descriptions
Use a stricter JSON schema validator that rejects non-conforming output and retries until valid
Add 2-3 few-shot examples showing the exact desired output format with correct field structure, consistent severity levels, and properly formatted suggested fixes
Post-process the output with a normalizer script that maps variant formats to the canonical structure
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Few-shot examples are the most effective technique for achieving consistently formatted output when detailed instructions alone produce inconsistent results.
[Why B fails]: Schema validation catches syntax errors but doesn't teach the model the correct format; retries without guidance produce the same inconsistencies.
[Why C fails]: More detailed instructions haven't worked (as stated) — adding more prose describing the format won't fix the problem.
[Why D fails]: Post-processing is brittle — it requires anticipating all variant formats and doesn't improve the model's output quality over time.
[Principle]: Few-shot examples are the most effective technique for output format consistency when instructions alone fail.
[Doc]: Prompt Engineering → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview
[Doc]: CI Structured Output → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/headless#get-structured-output

questions.question: How should you evaluate this proposal?
questions.scenario: Your team runs two Claude-powered workflows: (1) a blocking pre-merge security check that must complete before developers can merge PRs, and (2) a technical debt report generated overnight for the weekly team meeting. Your manager proposes switching both to the Message Batches API for its 50% cost savings.
options: Keep real-time calls for both to avoid batch result ordering complications
Use batch processing for the technical debt reports only; keep synchronous real-time API calls for the blocking pre-merge checks
Switch both to batch with a timeout fallback to real-time if batches take longer than 30 minutes
Switch both workflows to batch processing and poll for completion — the batch API usually finishes in minutes
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Batch API has up to 24-hour processing with no guaranteed latency SLA — unsuitable for blocking pre-merge checks but ideal for overnight non-blocking reports.
[Why B fails]: 'Usually finishes in minutes' isn't guaranteed; batch has no latency SLA, making it unacceptable for workflows that block developer merges.
[Why C fails]: Batch results are correlated via custom_id fields — ordering isn't a real problem. This reasoning misunderstands the API.
[Why D fails]: A 30-minute fallback adds complexity when the simpler solution is matching each workflow to its appropriate API based on latency requirements.
[Principle]: Match API approach to latency requirements: synchronous for blocking workflows, batch API for latency-tolerant ones.
[Doc]: Message Batches (API) → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/batch-processing

questions.question: What processing strategy is most appropriate for this SLA?
questions.scenario: Your team generates weekly code quality reports for 500 repositories. Each report requires one Claude API call. You have a 30-hour total SLA (reports must be ready by Monday 8am, submitted Friday evening). The Message Batches API has a 24-hour maximum processing window. Some batches occasionally fail for 5-10 repositories due to context length issues.
options: Split into 5 batches of 100 and submit them sequentially, each waiting for the previous to complete before starting the next
Process all 500 repositories with synchronous API calls on Friday night using parallel workers to ensure they complete within hours
Submit the batch Friday evening with 4-hour buffer before SLA deadline; resubmit only failed repositories (identified by custom_id) with modifications such as chunking oversized content
Submit the batch Thursday to guarantee completion by Monday, using a 48-hour buffer instead of the stated SLA
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: 30-hour SLA minus 24-hour batch window leaves ~6 hours for failure handling. Resubmitting failed documents by custom_id with modifications (chunking) is the standard recovery pattern.
[Why B fails]: Synchronous processing for 500 repos at full price when batch offers 50% savings wastes budget; the SLA easily accommodates batch timing.
[Why C fails]: Sequential batches multiply processing time (5 × up to 24h) instead of parallelizing; the SLA allows one batch run.
[Why D fails]: Submitting a day early reduces the buffer for addressing failures found after completion; the 30h SLA already provides sufficient buffer.
[Principle]: Calculate batch submission frequency from SLA constraints and handle failures by resubmitting modified documents using custom_id.
[Doc]: Message Batches (API) → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/batch-processing

questions.question: How should you restructure the review?
questions.scenario: A pull request modifies 14 files across the stock tracking module. Your single-pass review analyzing all files together produces inconsistent results: detailed feedback for some files but superficial comments for others, obvious bugs missed, and contradictory feedback — flagging a pattern as problematic in one file while approving identical code elsewhere.
options: Run three independent review passes on the full PR and only flag issues that appear in at least two of the three runs
Split into focused passes: analyze each file individually for local issues, then run a separate integration-focused pass examining cross-file data flow
Switch to a higher-tier model with a larger context window to give all 14 files adequate attention in one pass
Require developers to split large PRs into smaller submissions of 3-4 files before the automated review runs
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Multi-pass review — per-file local analysis plus cross-file integration passes — directly addresses attention dilution when processing many files at once.
[Why B fails]: Shifting burden to developers without improving the review system doesn't scale and creates friction.
[Why C fails]: Larger context windows don't solve attention quality; models still give inconsistent depth across 14 files.
[Why D fails]: Consensus filtering would suppress detection of real bugs that may only be caught intermittently.
[Principle]: Split large reviews into focused per-file passes plus separate integration passes to avoid attention dilution.
[Doc]: Multi-Pass Architecture → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works
[Doc]: Prompt Engineering – Focus → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview

questions.question: What review architecture would most effectively catch subtle issues?
questions.scenario: Your CI system uses Claude to generate code, then the same Claude session reviews its own output for bugs. Despite adding 'Now carefully review your code for errors' as a follow-up prompt, production bug rates haven't decreased. The system's self-reported confidence in its reviews is consistently high (8-9/10).
options: Increase the review prompt length with a comprehensive checklist of common bug patterns to look for
Add extended thinking with 'Think step by step about potential bugs' before the self-review to improve thoroughness
Have the original session re-read its generated code from a file (using the Read tool) rather than from context to simulate a fresh perspective
Use a second independent Claude instance (without the generator's reasoning context) to review the generated code in a fresh session
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Self-review is limited because the model retains reasoning context from generation, making it less likely to question its own decisions. Independent review instances are more effective.
[Why B fails]: Extended thinking in the same session still retains the generation context bias; the model is unlikely to contradict its recent reasoning.
[Why C fails]: A longer checklist doesn't address the fundamental bias of reviewing one's own work — the model generated the code for reasons it still believes in.
[Why D fails]: Re-reading from a file doesn't clear the reasoning context; the model still has its generation rationale in the conversation history.
[Principle]: Independent review instances (without prior reasoning context) are more effective than self-review in the same session.
[Doc]: Self-Review Architecture → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works
[Doc]: Session Isolation (SDK) → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/sessions

questions.question: What configuration ensures Claude Code has the right context?
questions.scenario: Your CI-invoked Claude Code is generating low-quality tests that duplicate existing test scenarios, use wrong assertion libraries, and don't follow your team's testing conventions (e.g., using factory fixtures instead of inline data). The test generation prompt says 'Write comprehensive tests for this function.'
options: Create a custom /test-gen slash command that includes all testing conventions inline in the command template
Document testing standards, valuable test criteria, assertion library choices, and available fixtures in the project's CLAUDE.md file so CI-invoked Claude Code inherits them automatically
Add --include-tests flag to include existing test files in Claude Code's context
Pass the complete test file directory contents in the -p prompt for every invocation
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: CLAUDE.md is the mechanism for providing project context to Claude Code in CI — it's loaded automatically for every invocation and contains persistent project conventions.
[Why B fails]: Passing full test directories in every prompt consumes tokens excessively and doesn't scale as the test suite grows.
[Why C fails]: Slash commands require interactive invocation; CI runs use -p flag which doesn't support slash commands.
[Why D fails]: --include-tests is not a real Claude Code flag.
[Principle]: CLAUDE.md provides persistent project context (testing standards, conventions, fixtures) to CI-invoked Claude Code.
[Doc]: CLAUDE.md & Memory → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/memory
[Doc]: CLAUDE.md in SDK → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/claude-code-features
[Doc]: CI Context – Headless Mode → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/headless
[Doc]: Test Generation Patterns → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/best-practices

questions.question: How should you handle re-runs after new commits?
questions.scenario: Your CI review pipeline runs on every push to a PR. After the initial review comments are posted, the developer pushes a fix commit. Running the review again on the updated code produces many of the same findings from the previous run (for unchanged code), cluttering the PR with duplicate comments alongside a few new findings for the changed code.
options: Delete all previous review comments before posting new ones to keep the PR clean
Add a deduplication step that compares new findings against existing comments by text similarity before posting
Include prior review findings in the new review's context, instructing Claude to report only new or still-unaddressed issues to avoid duplicate comments
Only run the review on the diff between the last reviewed commit and the new push
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Providing prior findings as context with instructions to report only new/unaddressed issues leverages the model's understanding to avoid duplicates intelligently.
[Why B fails]: Deleting previous comments removes the record of resolved issues and confuses developers tracking their fixes.
[Why C fails]: Reviewing only the diff misses cross-file issues where the fix introduced problems in related but unchanged code.
[Why D fails]: Text similarity deduplication is brittle — rephrased findings or findings about the same issue at a different line number won't match.
[Principle]: Include prior findings in context and instruct to report only new or still-unaddressed issues when re-running reviews.
[Doc]: Non-Interactive / Headless Mode → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/headless
[Doc]: GitHub Actions Integration → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/github-actions

questions.question: What output format configuration should you use?
questions.scenario: Your CI pipeline needs to post code review findings as inline comments on specific lines of a pull request. The downstream automation requires structured JSON with fields: file_path, line_number, severity, issue_description, and suggested_fix. Currently, Claude Code returns free-form markdown text that's difficult to parse reliably.
options: Add instructions in the prompt to 'respond only in JSON format' with the schema described in prose
Pipe the Claude Code output through a second Claude instance that converts markdown to the required JSON schema
Use --output-format json with --json-schema specifying the required fields to produce machine-parseable structured output
Parse the markdown output with regex to extract file paths, line numbers, and descriptions into the required JSON format
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: --output-format json with --json-schema are CLI flags designed specifically for enforcing structured output in CI contexts.
[Why B fails]: Regex parsing of free-form text is fragile and breaks when Claude's formatting changes even slightly.
[Why C fails]: Prose instructions for JSON format have inconsistent compliance — the model may include explanatory text, use wrong field names, etc.
[Why D fails]: A second Claude instance adds cost, latency, and another potential failure point when built-in structured output flags exist.
[Principle]: Use --output-format json and --json-schema CLI flags for structured CI output rather than parsing free-form text.
[Doc]: Forcing Tool Use for Structured Output → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/define-tools#forcing-tool-use
[Doc]: Structured Outputs (SDK) → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/structured-outputs
[Doc]: Tool Use – JSON Schema → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/overview
[Doc]: CLI Structured Output → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/headless#get-structured-output

questions.question: What approach most reliably guarantees schema-compliant output?
questions.scenario: You are building a data extraction system that must output structured JSON matching a strict schema with specific field names, types, and enum values. Currently, you use a prompt instruction asking Claude to respond in JSON format, but approximately 5% of responses contain JSON syntax errors (trailing commas, unquoted keys) or schema violations (wrong field names, missing required fields).
options: Wrap the prompt instruction with explicit examples of correct JSON and add 'IMPORTANT: Your response MUST be valid JSON'
Define an extraction tool with the JSON schema as its input parameters and use tool_use to extract the structured data from the tool call response
Use a regex-based parser that extracts JSON from the response text, fixing common syntax issues automatically
Add a JSON validator that catches syntax errors and retries the request up to 3 times until valid JSON is produced
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Tool use (tool_use) with JSON schemas is the most reliable approach for guaranteed schema-compliant output — it eliminates JSON syntax errors entirely.
[Why B fails]: Retry on syntax errors is wasteful when tool_use eliminates them by construction; retries also don't fix semantic schema violations.
[Why C fails]: Stronger prompt instructions reduce but don't eliminate the 5% failure rate; this is inherently probabilistic.
[Why D fails]: Regex-based fixers are fragile and can silently corrupt data; they handle syntax but not semantic violations.
[Principle]: Tool use with JSON schemas guarantees schema-compliant structured output, eliminating syntax errors by construction.
[Doc]: Tool Use (API) → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/overview
[Doc]: CLI Structured Output → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/headless#get-structured-output
[Doc]: Forcing Tool Use for Structured Output → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/define-tools#forcing-tool-use
[Doc]: Structured Outputs (SDK) → https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-sdk/structured-outputs

questions.question: How should you configure tool_choice for this system?
questions.scenario: Your extraction system processes documents of unknown type — some are invoices, some are contracts, some are resumes. You've defined three extraction tools: extract_invoice, extract_contract, and extract_resume, each with different JSON schemas. When tool_choice is set to 'auto', the model sometimes returns conversational text explaining it can't determine the document type instead of calling any extraction tool.
options: Set tool_choice to force extract_invoice specifically, since invoices are the most common document type
Add a prompt instruction: 'You MUST call one of the extraction tools. Never respond with text.'
Add a fourth tool called classify_document that the model calls first to determine the type before extraction
Set tool_choice to 'any' to guarantee the model calls one of the extraction tools rather than returning conversational text
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: tool_choice: 'any' guarantees the model calls a tool (but can choose which), preventing conversational fallback when document type is unknown.
[Why B fails]: Forcing a specific tool processes all documents as invoices regardless of actual type, producing garbage output for contracts and resumes.
[Why C fails]: Prompt instructions have non-zero failure rate; tool_choice: 'any' provides the deterministic guarantee that a tool is called.
[Why D fails]: An extra classification step adds latency and complexity; the model can select the appropriate extraction tool directly when forced to choose.
[Principle]: Use tool_choice: 'any' to guarantee structured output when multiple schemas exist and the input type is unknown.
[Doc]: tool_choice – Forcing Tool Use → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/define-tools#forcing-tool-use

questions.question: How should the schema be designed to prevent hallucination?
questions.scenario: Your extraction tool has a required field 'annual_revenue' in its JSON schema. When processing documents that don't mention revenue (e.g., a company profile without financial data), the model fabricates plausible-looking revenue figures to satisfy the required field constraint. This happens in approximately 15% of extractions.
options: Remove the annual_revenue field entirely from the schema since it's unreliable
Add a prompt instruction: 'If revenue information is not found, write "Not found" in the annual_revenue field'
Change 'annual_revenue' from a required field to an optional (nullable) field, allowing the model to return null when the information is not present in the source document
Add a validation step that checks if the extracted revenue value appears anywhere in the source document
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Making fields optional (nullable) when source documents may not contain the information prevents the model from fabricating values to satisfy required constraints.
[Why B fails]: String values like 'Not found' in a numeric field violate the schema type; also, the model may still hallucinate rather than admit absence.
[Why C fails]: Post-validation catches fabrication but doesn't prevent it; it adds a verification step when the schema design could prevent the issue.
[Why D fails]: Revenue is valuable when present — removing it entirely loses legitimate extractions to avoid 15% of bad ones.
[Principle]: Design schema fields as optional (nullable) when source documents may not contain the information, preventing fabrication.
[Doc]: Schema Design Practices → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/define-tools#best-practices-for-tool-definitions
[Doc]: Reducing Hallucination → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview

questions.question: What is the most effective retry strategy?
questions.scenario: Your extraction system processes invoices and validates that line item amounts sum to the stated total. In 8% of cases, validation fails — the extracted line items don't sum to the extracted total. Investigation shows these are format-related errors (e.g., the model misreads a table column alignment), not cases where the source document is actually inconsistent.
options: Switch to a different model that's better at table parsing rather than implementing retry logic
Retry the same request up to 3 times without modification, hoping for different output due to temperature variation
Skip validation for line item totals since the 8% error rate is acceptable for most use cases
Implement retry-with-error-feedback: send a follow-up request including the original document, the failed extraction, and the specific validation error explaining which values don't reconcile
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Retry-with-error-feedback gives the model specific information about what went wrong, enabling targeted self-correction on format/structural errors.
[Why B fails]: Blind retry without feedback relies on randomness; the model will likely make the same interpretation error repeatedly.
[Why C fails]: 8% error rate on financial data is significant; downstream systems relying on accurate totals would propagate errors.
[Why D fails]: The issue is table column alignment interpretation, which is a prompt/context problem — switching models doesn't address the systematic misread.
[Principle]: Retry-with-error-feedback guides model self-correction by including the failed extraction and specific validation errors.
[Doc]: Validation & Retry → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/handle-tool-calls#handling-errors-with-is_error

questions.question: When should you stop retrying?
questions.scenario: Your extraction system processes lease agreements. For 3% of documents, the 'lease_start_date' field consistently extracts as null even after retry-with-error-feedback. Investigation reveals these are verbal lease agreements transcribed from phone calls where the agent said 'we'll start whenever you're ready' — no specific date exists in the source. Your retry logic currently attempts up to 5 retries for these documents.
options: Try alternative extraction prompts that ask the model to infer a likely start date from other context in the document
Add an 'approximate_date' field alongside lease_start_date for cases where exact dates aren't available
Recognize that retries are ineffective when the information is simply absent from the source document; accept null for these cases and stop retrying after the first extraction confirms absence
Flag these documents for human review since the system cannot handle them automatically
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Retries are ineffective when information is genuinely absent — no amount of re-prompting can extract a date that doesn't exist in the source.
[Why B fails]: Inferring dates not stated in the document introduces hallucination; the system should represent what's in the source, not speculate.
[Why C fails]: Human review for missing data is expensive and unnecessary — the correct answer is null, which the system already produces.
[Why D fails]: An approximate date field still requires data that isn't there; it just labels the hallucination differently.
[Principle]: Stop retrying when information is genuinely absent from the source — distinguish missing data from extraction errors.
[Doc]: Retry Limits → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/handle-tool-calls#handling-errors-with-is_error
[Doc]: Handling Absent Information → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview

questions.question: What schema design pattern should you use?
questions.scenario: Your extraction system categorizes documents into types: 'invoice', 'contract', 'receipt', 'proposal'. However, you occasionally encounter document types outside this list — purchase orders, NDAs, and letters of intent. Currently, these get forced into the closest matching category, leading to schema violations when their fields don't match.
options: Remove the type field entirely and let the extraction schema handle any document format generically
Reject documents that don't match the predefined types and return an error rather than attempting extraction
Expand the enum to include all known document types: invoice, contract, receipt, proposal, purchase_order, NDA, letter_of_intent
Add an enum value 'other' alongside a detail string field (e.g., 'other_type_detail') for extensible categorization of documents outside the predefined list
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Enum + 'other' + detail string provides extensible categorization that handles unknown types gracefully without breaking the schema.
[Why B fails]: Expanding the enum to all known types doesn't handle future unknown types; you'll keep expanding forever as new types appear.
[Why C fails]: Removing the type field loses valuable metadata for downstream processing and routing decisions.
[Why D fails]: Rejecting unknown types means the system can't handle real-world document variety; it's too rigid for production.
[Principle]: Use enum with 'other' + detail string for extensible categories that handle unknown values gracefully.
[Doc]: Schema Design Practices → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/define-tools#best-practices-for-tool-definitions

questions.question: What should your validation strategy be before reducing human review?
questions.scenario: Your extraction system reports 97% overall accuracy across 10,000 documents processed last month. Management wants to automate high-confidence extractions entirely (no human review). However, you notice the 10,000 documents include 8,000 invoices (99% accuracy), 1,500 contracts (94% accuracy), and 500 government forms (82% accuracy).
options: Approve full automation since 97% aggregate accuracy exceeds the 95% threshold set by management
Automate all document types but add random spot-checking of 5% of outputs for ongoing quality monitoring
Validate accuracy by document type and field segment independently before automating — only automate document types that individually meet the accuracy threshold
Automate only invoices since they have the highest accuracy, and keep human review for all other types
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Aggregate accuracy (97%) masks poor performance on specific types (government forms at 82%). Each segment must independently meet thresholds before automation.
[Why B fails]: 97% aggregate hides that government forms have 18% error rate — automating these would produce unacceptable quality for that segment.
[Why C fails]: Random 5% sampling is unlikely to detect systematic failures in the 500-document government form segment; stratified sampling would be needed.
