[Why D fails]: Contracts at 94% might also be acceptable for automation depending on thresholds; the decision should be data-driven per type, not binary.
[Principle]: Aggregate accuracy metrics may mask poor performance on specific segments — validate per-type and per-field before automating.
[Doc]: Human-in-the-Loop Patterns → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works
[Doc]: Accuracy Segmentation → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/test-and-evaluate/develop-tests

questions.question: How should you implement confidence-based routing?
questions.scenario: Your extraction system processes 1,000 documents daily. You want to route only low-confidence extractions to human reviewers (limited to reviewing ~100 documents/day). The model outputs a confidence score with each extraction, but you're unsure if the scores are calibrated — a '95% confidence' extraction might actually be wrong 20% of the time.
options: Calibrate confidence thresholds using a labeled validation set: test the model's self-reported confidence against actual accuracy, then set routing thresholds based on empirical error rates
Trust the model's confidence scores directly — route anything below 95% confidence to human review
Implement stratified random sampling where 10% of all extractions (regardless of confidence) go to human review
Route all extractions with model confidence below 90% to human review since that leaves approximately 100 documents for reviewers
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Field-level confidence scores must be calibrated against labeled data before being used for routing — uncalibrated scores are unreliable proxies.
[Why B fails]: An arbitrary 90% threshold without calibration may route too many or too few documents; the threshold must be empirically validated.
[Why C fails]: Random sampling without confidence targeting doesn't prioritize limited reviewer capacity on documents most likely to contain errors.
[Why D fails]: Trusting uncalibrated confidence scores directly is the core problem stated — the model's '95% confidence' may correspond to 80% actual accuracy.
[Principle]: Calibrate confidence thresholds using labeled validation sets before using them for human review routing decisions.
[Doc]: Confidence & Routing → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/how-tool-use-works

questions.question: What tool_choice configuration ensures metadata is extracted first?
questions.scenario: Your document processing pipeline has two phases: (1) extract document metadata (type, date, author, page count) and then (2) run content-specific extraction tools based on the document type. You need to guarantee the metadata extraction happens before any content tools are called.
options: Add 'Always call extract_metadata first' to the system prompt and rely on the model to follow the instruction
Use tool_choice with forced tool selection: {"type": "tool", "name": "extract_metadata"} for the first API call, then switch to 'auto' for subsequent calls
Combine metadata and content extraction into a single tool to avoid ordering issues
List extract_metadata as the first tool in the tools array, since the model processes tools in order
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Forced tool selection (specific tool name in tool_choice) guarantees a particular tool is called first, then subsequent turns can use 'auto' for flexible selection.
[Why B fails]: Tool array order doesn't determine execution order — the model selects tools based on descriptions and context, not array position.
[Why C fails]: Prompt instructions have non-zero failure rates for ordering requirements; forced selection provides deterministic guarantees.
[Why D fails]: Combining tools loses the two-phase design benefit where metadata informs which content extraction to run.
[Principle]: Use forced tool selection to guarantee a specific tool is called first, then process subsequent steps in follow-up turns.
[Doc]: tool_choice – Forcing Tool Use → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/define-tools#forcing-tool-use

questions.question: What format normalization approach should you implement?
questions.scenario: Your extraction system processes documents from multiple sources. Dates appear in varied formats: 'January 15, 2025', '15/01/2025', '2025-01-15', '01-15-25'. Amounts appear as '$1,234.56', '1234.56 USD', '£1,234.56'. Your JSON schema expects ISO 8601 dates and numeric amounts in cents. Currently, 12% of extractions have format errors in these fields.
options: Reject documents with non-standard formats and ask users to resubmit in the expected format
Include format normalization rules in the extraction prompt alongside the output schema — specify that dates must be ISO 8601 and amounts must be integer cents regardless of source format
Create separate extraction tools for each source format (US dates, EU dates, ISO dates) and route documents to the matching tool
Post-process extracted values with a normalizer script that handles all known format variants
questions.explanation:
[Correct]: Including format normalization rules in the prompt alongside strict output schemas leverages the model's ability to interpret varied formats and produce consistent output.
[Why B fails]: Post-processing normalizers are fragile and require anticipating all format variants; new formats require code changes.
[Why C fails]: Rejecting varied formats is impractical for real-world documents that naturally use different conventions.
[Why D fails]: Creating tools per format is over-engineered and requires a format classification step; the model can handle normalization inline.
[Principle]: Include format normalization rules in prompts alongside strict output schemas to handle inconsistent source formatting.
[Doc]: Format Normalization in Tool Use → https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/tool-use/overview
